title: Plantain
subtitle: The soothing herb weed underfoot
excerpt: Plantago major, the common garden weed. A wound-supportive and soothing herb herb with a long record in western folk medicine for minor wounds, insect stings, mild eczema, and coughs. The most accessible first-aid herb in the British garden.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve (the early 20th-century botanical writer), A Modern Herbal, plantain entry: the wound, cough, and skin tradition, the leaf soothing gel and allantoin. Nicholas Culpeper (the 17th-century herbalist), The English Physician, plantain entry, the wound and cooling uses. King's American Dispensatory (Felter & Lloyd, 1898), Plantago major, the soothing herb and wound-supportive tradition.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herb that soothes and protects irritated or inflamed mucous membranes. Demulcents contain mucilage, a slippery substance that coats and calms inflamed tissue.
A herb that supports the skin in healing minor wounds and abrasions. Traditional terminology; not a medical claim.

content.text: Broadleaf plantain (Plantago major) is one of the most common plants in Britain, growing in lawns, paths, and verges. It forms a flat rosette of ribbed leaves and sends up slender spikes of tiny greenish flowers in summer. Despite its weed status, it has a long record in medicine. Culpeper (the 17th-century herbalist) calls it one of the most commonly-used herbs in English folk medicine. Ribwort plantain (Plantago lanceolata) is used the same way.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Plantain has no significant safety concerns at normal therapeutic doses. It is one of the safest herbs in the western tradition. Pregnancy: culinary and standard infusion doses are not a concern. Therapeutic syrups used over a prolonged period are outside the culinary safety envelope; discuss with a midwife or herbalist.

Foraging note. Broadleaf plantain (Plantago major) has no dangerous look-alikes in the UK. Ribwort plantain (Plantago lanceolata) is interchangeable for medicinal use. The ornamental plantain lily (Hosta species) looks different but is occasionally misidentified; Hosta leaves are smooth, not ribbed, and do not have the distinctive fibrous cords when torn.

content.text: Latin binomial: Plantago major. Family: Plantaginaceae. Parts used: leaf (fresh or dried). Harvest the young leaves before the plant flowers for infusions; older leaves are good for poultices and compresses. The ribbed leaves and strong fibrous cords running parallel to the edge are the identifying feature. Height 5-20 cm (the flowering spike reaches 30 cm). Year-round plant; the leaves are most potent in spring and early summer.
Allantoin (a compound found in comfrey leaf, linked to cell repair), soothing gel (the
component), aucubin (an iridoid glycoside with mild inflammation-calming activity), and tannins (the astringent component) are the key active compounds. The

action on minor skin wounds is attributed to the allantoin and soothing gel working together. The soothing herb action on coughs and irritated airways comes from the soothing gel. Tannins help stop minor bleeding, making the fresh poultice a traditional first aid for cuts and stings.

Maud Grieve (the early 20th-century botanical writer) records plantain as a wound herb, used for coughs and kidney complaints. Culpeper, the 17th-century herbalist, recommends it for wounds and insect stings. The King's American Dispensatory describes the North American Eclectic use for mucous membrane irritation, chronic mucus and congestion, and minor wounds.
Native American peoples used plantain (called "white man's foot" as it followed European settlers) for wounds, stings, and bites. This confirmed what European folk medicine had already established.

Fresh poultice: two or three leaves mashed between the palms (or bruised with a stone) and pressed onto a minor cut, graze, or insect sting. The oldest and most immediate form. Infusion for coughs: 2-4 g dried leaf in 200 ml water, steeped covered 10 minutes, with honey. Syrup: a strong infusion of dried leaf with honey (equal volumes infusion and honey), strained and stored in the fridge. Compress: strong infusion cooled to skin-tolerable, cloth soaked and applied to irritated skin. Dried leaf infusions are mildly less effective than fresh-leaf preparations.

Plantain is among the safest herbs in domestic use. No significant drug interactions are documented. Any cough persisting beyond 3 weeks, any cough with blood-streaked mucus, any cough with significant breathlessness, or any wound that will not close or shows signs of infection needs medical assessment.
